coredata.dc:title: Sustainable preparation of luminescent carbon dots from syringe waste and hyaluronic acid for cellular imaging and antimicrobial applications
coredata.dc:creator: Nangan, Senthilkumar
Kanagaraj, Kuppusamy
Kaarthikeyan, Gurumoorthy
Kumar, Anuj
Ubaidullah, Mohd
Pandit, Bidhan
Govindasamy, Rajakumar
Natesan, Thirumalaivasan
coredata.dc:description: Addressing the global challenge of persistent waste through an eco-conscious strategy to transform it into valuable and versatile materials holds great significance in today's swiftly evolving world. By adopting a sustainable approach, we can repurpose waste syringes composed of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) into fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using a simple hydrothermal process. This research harnessed hyaluronic acid to carbonize and modify discarded plastic syringes, resulting in the creation of luminescent syringe carbon dots (SCDs). Rigorous analysis employing diverse techniques delved into their optical attributes, size distribution, and surface characteristics. Extensive biocompatibility assessments using established assay methods confirmed the safety of the derived SCDs, unveiling their potential antibacterial and antifungal traits. Additionally, a confocal microscope was employed to evaluate the cellular imaging capabilities of SCDs on HeLa cells. Notably, at bactericidal concentrations, SCDs exhibited mild cytotoxicity towards mammalian cells, showcasing cell viability surpassing 91.07% at 1 mg/mL. This pioneering exploration paves the way for potential applications of SCD-based nano-bactericides across various biomedical domains. The initial outcomes established herein mark a significant stride towards the creation of cost-effective and ecologically sound fluorescent probes for biomedical imaging, aimed at combating microbial infections. By ingeniously reutilizing polyethylene terephthalate (PET), this investigation offers a sustainable remedy to address the ecological predicaments linked with plastic waste. In doing so, it charts a course towards contributing to the development of affordable, eco-friendly solutions, heralding a promising prospect for a cleaner, healthier environment.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) and b) SEM images of SCDs, Scale bar 1 μm and 0.5 μm, respectively. c) depict HR-TEM image of SCDs, with scale bar of 50 nm. (The inset shows the closer view of the HR-TEM image of SCDs). d) average particle size of SCDs, as observed in (c).
Characterization of SCDs using a) X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, b) FT-IR spectra, and c) EDX analysis.

a) The optical characteristics of SCDs were analyzed through UV–vis absorption (red line) and fluorescence emission (blue line) spectra. b) The fluorescent emission spectra of the SCDs were investigated with varying excitation wavelengths between 320 and 460 nm to study the excitation dependence.

Antibacterial activity by the well diffusion method: Representative images of agar plates containing CDs impregnated disks and zone of inhibition for SCDs along with (1) staphylococcus aureus (2) streptococcus mutans, (3) E coli and (4) klebsiella pneumoniae, respectively. a. control (streptomycin for + Ve and ampicillin for -Ve) b. SCDs (50 μg), c. SCDs (100 μg).
Antioxidant activity of the SCDs at different concentrations: SCDs (red bar), and Ascorbic acid (green bar).

The CLSM images depicted the biofilms of Streptococcus mutans that were cultivated for 48 h and subjected to a 12-h treatment with different samples. The biofilms were categorized into three groups: a) the control group, b) the group treated with 1 mg/mL of SCDs, and c) the group treated with 2 mg/mL of SCDs. In the images, live cells were represented by the green color (acridine orange). The scale bars in the images corresponded to a length of 250 μm.
Viability of HeLa cells under different concentrations of SCDs, with DMSO used as a reference control (no of runs, n = 3), a comparative analysis was conducted.
Confocal fluorescence images of SCDs stained HeLa cells. (a) λex = 380 nm, (b) λex = 440 nm, (c) λex = 540 nm, (d) Overlay image. Scale bar: 10 μm.

Schematic representation of the hydrothermal method is utilized to synthesize syringe-based syringe carbon dots, SCDs incorporating hyaluronic acid, enabling their use in antimicrobial and biomarker applications.
Comparative Analysis of preparation and application of contemporary Luminescent carbon dots.

row.entry: Detection of Cu2+, Fe3+and Hg2+ ions
Detection of Fe3+ ion and Pyrophosphate anion
Plastic waste from bags, cups and bottles
250 °C, 5 h and 180 °C in 4% aqueous H2O2 for 12 h

entry.bold: PET Syringe Waste and Hyaluronic Acid

head.ce:title: Sustainable preparation of luminescent carbon dots from syringe waste and hyaluronic acid for cellular imaging and antimicrobial applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Addressing the global challenge of persistent waste through an eco-conscious strategy to transform it into valuable and versatile materials holds great significance in today's swiftly evolving world. By adopting a sustainable approach, we can repurpose waste syringes composed of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) into fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using a simple hydrothermal process. This research harnessed hyaluronic acid to carbonize and modify discarded plastic syringes, resulting in the creation of luminescent syringe carbon dots (SCDs). Rigorous analysis employing diverse techniques delved into their optical attributes, size distribution, and surface characteristics. Extensive biocompatibility assessments using established assay methods confirmed the safety of the derived SCDs, unveiling their potential antibacterial and antifungal traits. Additionally, a confocal microscope was employed to evaluate the cellular imaging capabilities of SCDs on HeLa cells. Notably, at bactericidal concentrations, SCDs exhibited mild cytotoxicity towards mammalian cells, showcasing cell viability surpassing 91.07% at 1 mg/mL. This pioneering exploration paves the way for potential applications of SCD-based nano-bactericides across various biomedical domains. The initial outcomes established herein mark a significant stride towards the creation of cost-effective and ecologically sound fluorescent probes for biomedical imaging, aimed at combating microbial infections. By ingeniously reutilizing polyethylene terephthalate (PET), this investigation offers a sustainable remedy to address the ecological predicaments linked with plastic waste. In doing so, it charts a course towards contributing to the development of affordable, eco-friendly solutions, heralding a promising prospect for a cleaner, healthier environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymeric materials, exemplified by Polyethylene terephthalate (PET), represent colorless and semi-crystalline resins that have found extensive utility across diverse industries. These materials owe their widespread adoption to their remarkable physical and chemical attributes, which render them highly suitable for serving as containers for medicines, food, and soft drinks (Andrew and Dhakal, 2022; Evode et al., 2021; Naser et al., 2021; Sarda et al., 2022). The commendable traits of PET encompass elevated thermal stability, flexibility, transparency, waterproofing, effective insulation, minimal electrical conductivity, and robust resistance to acids, sunlight, and microorganisms (Yaqoob et al., 2022). Additionally, PET offers economic feasibility, facile handling, lightweight nature, and substantial strength and durability, all of which further augment its widespread acceptance (Samir et al., 2022). Regrettably, the escalating global consumption of plastics, coupled with inefficient waste management practices and suboptimal recycling rates, has engendered significant environmental predicaments.

Primarily within the medical domain, often within healthcare centers and hospitals, the practicality of the ubiquitous thermoplastic PET material is remarkably extensive. It finds utility in various applications, including sutures, syringes, implantable textiles, heart valves, surgical meshes, scaffolds, urinary and bloodstream catheters, electrosurgical instrument tips, tubing for angioplasty balloons, injection-molded components such as connectors for pulse oximeters, and clips for breathing masks. However, the safety and reusability of these components have been a cause for concern (Ncube et al., 2021; Peleg and Hooper, 2010). Despite the presence of numerous reusable techniques, the unpredictable and often unnoticed challenges persist in this field. In recent times, there is a pressing need to address the predicaments arising from PET usage. A proactive approach towards repurposing PET into valuable materials for diverse applications, while being environmentally conscious, is of paramount importance in today's swiftly evolving world (Moshood et al., 2022). PET, ranking as the fourth most produced plastic on a global scale, compounds this challenge by contributing to an annual production exceeding 30 million tons, coupled with a low reclamation rate (Kibria et al., 2023; Worm et al., 2017). Consequently, it becomes imperative to explore ingenious solutions to combat the escalating waste volumes and foster sustainability. A promising avenue involves harnessing discarded syringes from healthcare establishments as a valuable reservoir for producing fluorescent carbon dots, denoted as CDs. This approach not only aids in waste reduction but also facilitates the generation of valuable nanomaterials. By converting discarded syringes into fluorescent carbon dots, referred to as SCDs, we can effectively address the environmental apprehensions linked to PET waste and contribute substantively to the advancement of sustainable practices.

Dang et al. (2016) introduced a unique and innovative procedure for generating carbon dots with specific sensing capabilities. Their method utilized waste plastic as a valuable organic precursor, emphasizing its potential as a sustainable source. In the two-step process, the PET plastic was initially subjected to air oxidation before proceeding to undergo hydrothermal treatment. By adopting this approach, not only is plastic waste efficiently converted but it also contributes to the advancement of sustainable materials and circular economy practices(Arpita et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2022). While previous research has explored limited techniques for obtaining carbon dots from waste plastic, our study aimed to expand the range of potential applications by synthesizing fluorescent CDs (Thirumalaivasan and Wu, 2020; Venkatesan et al., 2023). We used syringe waste and hyaluronic acid as starting materials. Our investigation focused on evaluating the potential use of these SCDs in cellular imaging and antimicrobial assays, which revealed that the synthesized SCDs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility and were capable of effectively imaging cell lines. Furthermore, standardized assay methods were employed to evaluate the antimicrobial and antifungal properties of SCDs. Encouragingly, the results indicated that SCDs exhibited efficacy against a wide range of microorganisms, including drug-resistant strains. These findings highlight the potential of synthesized SCDs as promising therapeutic agents. By repurposing waste plastics and exploring their applications in the form of carbon dots, our study contributes to advancing the utilization of plastic waste and highlights its potential in the fields of cellular imaging and antimicrobial research. Hence, the primary objective of this research was to devise an innovative technique for producing luminescent carbon dots utilizing discarded syringes and hyaluronic acid. This approach not only optimizes waste utilization but also extends to applications in cellular imaging and antimicrobial capabilities, marking a significant stride forward in the realm of carbon dot synthesis and its practical uses. The research centered on eco-conscious, economical SCDs derived from syringe waste and hyaluronic acid, underscoring their potential in the realms of biomedical imaging and antimicrobial applications. This approach is in harmony with waste management principles and advances environmentally friendly nanomaterials in biotechnology and healthcare. Thorough characterization and testing were conducted on the SCDs to assess their imaging and antimicrobial potentials, imparting meaningful implications for cost-effective probes and combatting infections.

The investigation made use of syringes obtained from the medical waste of Saveetha Dental College, Saveetha University, Chennai, India. Deionized water was used as the dispersion solvent to prepare the samples. High-quality ethanol with a purity of 99%, sourced from Fisher-Scientific, was used for antimicrobial experiments. The Muller Hinton agar and nutrient broth utilized for antimicrobial testing were acquired from HiMedia, ensuring a purity level of 99%.

The aim of this study was to synthesize fluorescent SCDs using discarded medical syringes obtained from Saveetha Dental College, which are classified as disposable plastic waste (Scheme 1 ). To initiate the process, plastic waste, including syringes, was thoroughly washed to eliminate any adhesive substances. After the strains/contaminations/waste was effectively cleaned, it was placed into a ceramic crucible. Thermal calculations were then conducted for a duration of 2 h at 400 °C (from RT increased by 20 °C per min.) inside a muffle furnace. After that the muffle furnace temperature is reduced to RT by dissenting the heating rate of 10 °C per minutes.

After synthesis, the resulting powders exhibited a black hue and were allowed to return to the ambient temperature. The samples were mechanically crushed to obtain the powdered product. In order to prepare SCDs, 1 g of carbonized PET powdered product into a 15 mL of a 3% H2O2 solution containing Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The entire setup was subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 5 h. To eliminate larger residues, the SCD dispersion obtained was subjected to centrifuged for 15 min at 7000 rpm. The resulting concentrated solution was then subjected to dialysis using a 1 kDa MWCO membrane. This process lasted for 3 days. Ultracentrifugation at 15,000 rpm/freeze drying method was performed to transform the dispersed SCDs into a solid SCDs.

A comprehensive examination of the size and structure of SCDs was carried out utilizing scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Jeol JSM IT 800), ensuring originality in the analysis. To determine the size and crystal phases of the synthesized SCDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was employed. The freeze-dried SCDs underwent analysis using a Bruker FTIR spectrometer under standard atmospheric conditions. Subsequently, the samples were scanned across a wavenumber range of 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1. To obtain the UV–visible spectra of SDC dissolved in ultrapure water, a V-630 Bio spectrometer from jasco, a Japanese manufacturer, was utilized. The measurements were conducted at room temperature using a quartz cell with a 1 cm optical path. The absorption spectra were obtained within a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Elemental composition analysis of the SCDs was performed using an EDX device. Furthermore, an elemental analyzer from Oxford Instruments was employed to determine the levels of carbon (C), and oxygen (O) present in the SCDs.

An investigation was conducted to assess the antimicrobial properties of SCDs derived from recycled single use plastic syringe. The study involved testing both Gram-positive and Gram-negative microorganisms using two different antibacterial analysis methods: There are two methods commonly used in the field, namely the minimal zone of inhibition assay and the agar well diffusion method. To initiate the experiment, cultures of staphylococcus aureus, streptococcus mutans, Escherichia coli and klebsiella pneumoniae were cultivated in nutrient broth. For the positive control, bacterial cultures were mixed with deionized water. Biocompatibility was investigated using different concentrations of SCDs (500, 1000, and 5000 ppm). The agar solution was subjected to autoclaved at 120 °C for 2 h to maintain sterile conditions. Next, approximately 25 mL of Muller-Hinton agar was carefully poured into individual petri dishes. Subsequently, the plates were subjected to an incubation period of 24 h at a temperature of 37 °C within a laminar airflow cabinet. Using a bacterial loop, untainted bacterial strains were cautiously spread onto the appropriate Petri plates. Afterwards, the plates were punctured with a puncher, creating wells with a diameter of 5 mm. Each well was subsequently filled with a freshly prepared solution of SCDs, measuring 10 μL, with varying concentrations. Following this, the plates were once again incubated at 37 °C for another 24 h. In subsequent investigations, the reduction in bacterial population was assessed for further analysis.

To assess the cytotoxicity of the nanoconjugates and SCDs against HeLa cell lines, the MTT test was employed. HeLa cells were seeded at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well in a 96 well plate. The plate was then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2 to allow cell attachment and growth. After the initial incubation, the culture media was replaced with fresh medium containing different concentrations of the SCDs. The cells were further incubated for an additional 24 h to expose them to the test compounds. To evaluate cell viability, each well was supplemented with 70 μL of medium, 30 μL of MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) solution at a concentration of 1 mg/mL. Subsequently, the mixture was incubated for 4 h. After the incubation period, 100 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well to dissolve the purple formazan crystals formed by viable cells. The plate was further incubated for 10 min to ensure complete dissolution. The absorbance of the resulting solution was then measured at a wavelength of 570 nm using an ELISA plate reader. Based on these measurements, the percentage of the cell viability was estimated using a standard method.

PET, a thermoplastic polyester renowned for its remarkable thermal stability and resistance to mineral acids, oxidizing agents, sunlight, and microorganisms in normal environmental conditions, was effectively converted into fluorescent SCDs through an environmentally friendly two-step synthetic approach. This innovative method involved employing air thermal oxidation and hydrothermal techniques. Initially, the waste PET was treated in an open-air environment at a temperature of 400 °C for a duration of 2 h. This step induced cross-linking and discoloration of the polymer, resulting in the formation of a dark brown oxidized product. The oxidized product exhibited diverse chromophoric systems and abundant oxygen-containing groups, making it significantly more reactive than PET. Subsequently, the oxidized product underwent hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C, facilitating its transformation into SDCs. The synthesis conditions were carefully optimized to achieve the desired results, with temperature being identified as a critical parameter. It was found that 400 °C was considered suitable to strike a balance between structural breakdown and product yield.

The structural features of the synthesized SCDs were probed using SEM, revealing aggregated structures composed of nearly spherical-shaped particles (Fig. 1 a and b). Further insight into the structural nature was obtained through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analysis, revealing homogenous dispersion of SCDs (Fig. 1c). Furthermore, investigation of SCDs in an aqueous medium unveiled an average particle size ranging from 3 to 7 nm (Fig. 1d), consistent with findings by Sun et al. (2023); Liu et al. (2023). The amorphous aspects were assessed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), where a prominent broad peak at 2θ of 22.3 indicated the presence of a graphitic carbon structure (Fig. 2 a). This observation aligns with the studies of Alexpandi et al., (2020), Döring et al., (2022); Das et al. (2022), which emphasized a similar characteristic peak associated with graphitic carbon.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 2b) vividly illustrates distinct absorption bands that signify various vibrational modes. Noteworthy vibrations encompass O–H stretching vibrations at 3440 cm−1, C–H stretching vibrations at 2925 cm−1, C]O stretching vibrations at 1712 cm−1, and C–O–C bending vibrations at 1409 cm−1. These observations align with findings in the works of Ghahremani et al. (2023), Pandiyan et al., (2020); Sailaja Prasannakumaran Nair et al., (2020), thus establishing a foundation of agreement with contemporary studies. The energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis exposes the elemental composition of the synthesized SCDs, revealing that the primary constituents were carbon (C) at 58.8% and oxygen (O) at 41.2%, as depicted in Fig. 2c. This composition aligns with the findings in Hazarika and Karak (2016) and Khaledian et al. (2021) lending further credence to the characterization. These results collectively indicate that the SCDs are primarily composed of a carbon structure enriched with oxygen. The amalgamation of all characterization data leads to the conclusive assertion that the synthesized materials can be classified as SCDs. These SCDs exhibit a structure reminiscent of graphite and exhibit a notable presence of oxygen functional groups on their surfaces.

When the SCDs were dissolved in water, the resulting solution exhibited transparency and emitted a vibrant blue fluorescence when exposed to a UV lamp (refer to Fig. 3 a, insets). The measurement of excitation and transmitted optical bandwidth was conducted at a narrow interval of 3.0 nm. In all UV–Vis and emission spectroscopy analysis, a consistent concentration of 0.05 mg/mL of SCDs was utilized. The UV–visible absorption spectra of the SCDs, shown in Fig. 3a, displayed a broad band around 240 nm, indicating the presence of an n-π* transition associated with the C]O groups(Feghhi et al., 2023; Aladesuyi and Oluwafemi, 2023). Specifically, under an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, a strong fluorescent peak at 421 nm (blue line) was observed in the aqueous solution. Furthermore, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the SCDs revealed that their emission behavior was dependent on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 3b). When excited at 320 nm, the SCDs exhibited a maximum PL peak at 434 nm. However, as the excitation wavelength was increased from 320 nm to 460 nm while the intensity was gradually decreased. This variation can be attributed to the presence of different surface emissive traps and the varying sizes of the SCDs. The quantum yield was measured to be 5.2% using anthracene as a standard (refer to the supplementary material for the detailed calculation method). In summary, the above findings demonstrate that the synthesized SCDs possess excellent optical properties. Their ability to produce a vibrant blue fluorescent when dissolved in water, along with their tunable emission behavior based on excitation wavelength, indicates their potential for diverse applications. These results provide valuable insights and lay the foundation for further exploration and utilization of SCDs in various applications.

In recent days, formation of free radical by our body due to external factors such as X-rays, ozone layer destruction, alcohol, air pollutants and unhealthy lifestyle pose serious threat to the most essential biochemical components such as proteins, lipids, DNA and leads to oxidative stress (Fussell and Kelly, 2020; Zhang et al., 2019). This in turn causes several diseases viz. Ageing, CVD, obesity and neurological disorders. Therefore, supplementation of antioxidants has become the need of hour to combat the oxidative stress. In this study, the antioxidant capability of SCDs was evaluated and compared to previously reported CDs with established antioxidant properties. The DPPH inhibition is the most reliable methods to assess the antioxidant potentials of the SCDs, which involves the interaction between the free radical DPPH and the antioxidant present in the sample. In the present investigation, the results of DPPH showed dose-dependent increase in scavenging DPPH radicals (Fig. 5 ). For instance, at 25 μg/mL, SCDs exhibited 23.95% radical scavenging property, whereas ascorbic acid displayed a higher rate of 39.7%. But, both SCDs and positive control showed a steady increase in quenching DPPH radicals with respect to raise in concentration and recorded a maximum of 72.2% and 82.85% at higher concentration (500 μg/mL).

This trend infers that SCDs possess promising proton (H+) donating ability and thereby suggest presence of functional groups such as carboxylic acids, hydroxyl on the surface of SCDs. Supporting the results of present study, Gedda et al. (2023) showed the promising DPPH radical potentials of carbon dots biosynthesized from the leaves of Azadirachta indica (Gedda et al., 2023; Similarly, Murru et al., 2020 demonstrated potent DPPH radical quenching ability of SCDs obtained from tea wastes (Murru et al., 2020). Hence, the observed strong antioxidant activity of our Luminescent Carbon Dots from Syringe Waste and Hyaluronic Acid can be attributed to their unique carbon nanostructure, which likely facilitates efficient electron transfer and radical scavenging. This behavior is likely enhanced by the presence of functional groups from both waste-derived carbon and hyaluronic acid, synergistically contributing to their potent antioxidant capacity.

To better understand the effects of SCDs on the development of streptococcus mutants biofilms, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was utilized. This allowed to investigate different concentrations of SCDs affected the removal and permeability of these biofilms, using acridine orange labeling. The control group, which did not receive any SCDs treatment, exhibited a densely formed biofilm. This observation was further supported by visual examination, where the biofilm appeared to have a greater thickness. However, when it is treated with SCDs at concentrations of 1 mg/mL and 2 mg/mL, a significant reduction in the number of biofilms attached to the dish's surface has observed, and this reduction became more pronounced as the concentration of SCDs increased. Additionally, based on the biofilm using fluorescence intensity, provided further evidence of reduced biofilm formation once the concentration reached 2 mg/mL of SCDs (Fig. 6 c). These findings clearly demonstrate that SCDs possess an impressive ability to inhibit the formation of biofilms. The Cytotoxicity of SCDs was assessed using HeLa cells through an MTT assay. Fig. 7 illustrates that the cellular viability remains 91.07% even at a high concentration of 1 mg/mL, suggesting that SDCs possess minimal toxicity and high biocompatibility.

Additionally, SCDs were employed in cell-imaging experiments, revealing the presence of blue, green, and red fluorescence in HeLa cells when excited at wavelengths of 380 nm, 440 nm, and 540 nm respectively (Fig. 8 ). This outcome demonstrated the favorable cell permeability of SCDs, offering their effectiveness as imaging probes.

In conclusion, this study demonstrates the sustainable synthesis of luminescent carbon dots (SCDs) through the innovative utilization of syringe waste and hyaluronic acid. Various analysis methods (UV, Fluorescence, FT-IR, SEM, HR-TEM, XRD, EDX) were employed to characterize the SCDs, revealing strong fluorescence and water solubility attributed to surface functional groups. This environmentally conscious approach fosters advanced nanomaterial preparation, negating the need for expensive materials and harsh conditions. Notably, at bactericidal concentrations, SCDs exhibited mild cytotoxicity towards mammalian cells, showcasing cell viability surpassing 91% at 2 mg/mL. These SCDs exhibit remarkable luminescent properties, making them highly suitable for cellular imaging applications. The incorporation of upcycled syringe waste contributes to waste reduction and environmentally conscious nanomaterial production. The successful integration of hyaluronic acid further enhances the biocompatibility and potential antimicrobial applications of the SCDs. The synthesized SCDs present promising prospects for advancing cellular imaging techniques and addressing antimicrobial challenges in a sustainable manner and plastic waste mitigation.

Senthilkumar Nangan: Methodology, Investigation. Kuppusamy Kanagaraj: Writing – original draft. Gurumoorthy Kaarthikeyan: Reviewing. Anuj Kumar: Formal analysis. Mohd Ubaidullah: Validation. Bidhan Pandit: Data curation, Investigation. Rajakumar Govindasamy: Formal analysis. Thirumalaivasan Natesan: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSPD2023R682), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for the support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) for sensitive and selective determination of mercury (Hg2+) in Oreochromis niloctus (Tilapia fish)
Inorg. Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Aladesuyi, O. A., Oluwafemi, O. S., 2023. Synthesis of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) for sensitive and selective determination of mercury (Hg2+) in Oreochromis niloctus (Tilapia fish). Inorganic Chemistry Communications. 153, 110843.
